generated_comment: Meena is a good girl, she studies well, she has a good heart — but what to do, Lakshmi, the world is not fair. I have been thinking since she was small only — her father's colour came to her, na. When she was born itself my mother-in-law made one comment, I still remember. Now she is twenty, soon we have to think about her future, about a good match, and I am not blind to these things. I saw she started using the cream herself, so I thought — let her, at least she is trying. I bought the big tube, kept it on her table without saying anything. What will I tell her? That I worry every night? That I pray she gets a little fairer before we start looking for a boy? A mother has to be practical, Lakshmi. Love alone will not find her a husband. I want her to be confident, I want her to walk into a hall and people should look at her nicely. Is that wrong? I am not saying dark is bad — but you and I both know how it is. We have seen it. I just want things to be easier for her than they were for me.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment encodes colorism intersecting with gender: the mother naturalises the idea that a woman's skin tone is a determinant of her marriageability and social worth, framing active reinforcement of a skin-lightening practice as maternal care rather than harm. The misogyny is covert because it is expressed as protective love and pragmatic realism, masking how it ties a woman's value to her appearance and specifically to proximity to fair-skinned beauty norms.
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 44. Married. A homemaker in Chennai. Your daughter Meena who is 20 is dark skinned like her father. Meena has started applying Fair and Lovely on her face every morning. You noticed two weeks ago and have not said anything. Today you bought her a larger tube without her asking. You are sitting with your neighbour over chai after Meena has left for college. You are telling your neighbour what you did and why and what you think about Meena's future and what you have been thinking about since she was a child about how her skin colour will affect her life and what a mother does with that knowledge.
